title: The GRAMMYs 2026: Song Of The Year Nominees Analysis
text:
The GRAMMYs 2026: Song Of The Year Nominees Analysis

Overview: Eight nominees compete for Song Of The Year, awarded to songwriters for melody and lyrics first released during the eligibility period. The previous winner was "Not Like Us" by Kendrick Lamar.

Nominees Summary:

WILDFLOWER (Billie Eilsch) — Winner
Acoustic-driven ballad from her album Hit Me Hard and Soft, co-written with brother Finneas. Critics praised its "emotional restraint and cinematic sweep," with Rolling Stone noting it "builds slowly into one of the album's most affecting moments."

Abracadabra (Lady Gaga)
Nostalgic pop track from Mayhem featuring an interpolation of Siouxsie and the Banshees' "Spellbound." The song employs a "gibberish chorus reminiscent of 'Bad Romance'" and drew comparisons to her early career work.

Anxiety (Doechii)
Hip-hop track sampling Gotye and Kimbra's "Somebody That I Used to Know." Billboard highlighted its "viral-turned-radio hit" status with crossover success across multiple airplay charts.

APT. (ROSÉ, Bruno Mars)
Lead single from ROSÉ's debut album, inspired by a Korean drinking game. YouTube's fifth-fastest video to reach one billion views, praised for bridging "cultures and genres" as a "global pop anthem."

DtMF (Bad Bunny)
Genre-blending anthem fusing Puerto Rican plena with modern pop/reggaeton. Rolling Stone called its success "a cultural movement" that brought traditional Afro-Puerto Rican music to global audiences.

Golden (HUNTR/X: EJAE, Audrey Nuna, REI AMI)
Netflix's KPop Demon Hunters theme song blending K-pop songwriting with musical theater expertise. Billboard described it as "rich with lyrical meaning" featuring "gargantuan melodies."

luther (Kendrick Lamar featuring SZA)
Love song sampling Luther Vandross and Cheryl Lynn's "If This World Were Mine." NPR noted Lamar's vocal performance on this "hip-hop ballad," performed at Super Bowl LIX Halftime Show.

Manchildh (Sabrina Carpenter)
Lead single from Man's Best Friend offering sharp social commentary. The Guardian praised its "construction and stickiness," while the song sparked viral TikTok dance trends.
